title: Esterházy torte
subtitle: Hungarian walnut layer cake with mocha buttercream and feathered fondant
excerpt: Esterházy torte is a Hungarian celebration cake: five thin, crisp walnut meringue discs layered with a custard mocha buttercream, glazed with apricot jam, and topped with white fondant pulled into the characteristic feathered web pattern with dark chocolate. The layers are delicate when fresh and firm up overnight into something closer to nougat. It rewards patience; assembly takes time but the result keeps for days.
sourceNotes: Esterházy torte is named after Prince Paul III Anton Esterházy de Galántha (1786-1866), one of the great patrons of Austro-Hungarian court culture. The cake was created by a Budapest confectioner in his honour and became a fixture of Central European patisserie. It is listed as a traditional Hungarian cake alongside Dobos torte and Rigó Jancsi. The feathered fondant pattern is a signature of the Vienna school of pastry.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The walnut layers can be baked and stored airtight at room temperature for 2 days. The assembled cake keeps refrigerated for 4 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classic patisserie decoration in which parallel lines of dark chocolate are piped across a white fondant surface while the fondant is still fluid, then a skewer or cocktail stick is drawn alternately through the lines in opposite directions to create a chevron or spider-web pattern. The technique requires the fondant to be fluid enough to move when the skewer pulls through it — typically at about 35 degrees C. Associated with Esterházy torte, Dobos torte, and Central European patisserie.

content.text: The walnut layers are a nut meringue; no yeast, no chemical leavening, just stiffly whisked egg whites folded with ground walnuts, a little flour, and sugar. They bake quickly and come out as thin, crisp discs. They are fragile when warm; move them off the parchment only once cool. The top of the cake is the characteristic
: poured white fondant with chocolate lines drawn into a web. Work quickly; the fondant sets in a few minutes.

items.prepNote: finely ground in a food processor

content.text: Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Draw five 20 cm circles on baking parchment; two per sheet where possible. Whisk 6 egg whites to stiff peaks in a very clean bowl. Gradually add 220 g caster sugar, whisking to a firm, glossy meringue. Fold in 220 g finely ground walnuts and 50 g flour until just combined. Divide the mixture between the five circles and spread to an even layer within each circle. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes per batch until lightly coloured and dry to the touch. Cool completely on the parchment before peeling off.

Heat 300 ml milk to just below simmering. Whisk 4 egg yolks with 100 g sugar, 30 g cornflour, and 2 tsp espresso powder. Pour the hot milk over the yolk mixture in a thin stream, whisking. Return to medium heat and stir constantly until thick and bubbling, about 3 minutes. Press cling film onto the surface and cool completely. Beat 200 g softened butter until pale. Add the cold custard in spoonfuls, beating after each until the buttercream is smooth and fluffy.

Place one walnut layer on a serving plate. Spread about a quarter of the buttercream over it. Repeat with the remaining layers, ending with a plain layer. Press gently to level. Brush the top and sides with the warm sieved apricot jam. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set the jam.

Gently warm 250 g fondant icing over a bain-marie, stirring, until it is fluid and about 35 degrees C; it should pour slowly. Working quickly, pour it over the top of the cake and spread to the edges. Pipe or drizzle parallel lines of melted dark chocolate across the white fondant, spacing them about 2 cm apart. Drag a skewer or cocktail stick through the lines perpendicular to them, alternating direction with each pass, to create the feathered web. Chill for at least 1 hour before slicing with a hot knife.

items.problem: Walnut layers cracked when peeling off the parchment
items.solution: The layers were moved before they were completely cool, or baked on parchment that was not greased lightly. Cool fully on the paper before attempting to peel. Run a palette knife under the edge to loosen gently.

The custard was added too warm, or the butter was too cold. Both the butter and custard should be at room temperature — about 20 degrees C — before combining. If it splits, warm the bowl briefly over hot water and beat again.

items.problem: Fondant set before the feathered pattern was complete
items.solution: The fondant cooled too fast. Work at room temperature, not in a cold kitchen. If the fondant sets before you finish, gently warm the surface with a cook's blowtorch or the back of a warm spoon to re-liquefy, then continue.
